data.title: Robinhood: A Strong Business At A Difficult Entry Point
data.description:
Introduction:

Robinhood (HOOD) has continued to evolve from a simple brokerage platform into an all-in-one digital finance ecosystem with banking services, prediction markets, tokenized equities, and institutional-grade crypto trading features.

The company's recent initiatives have significantly strengthened its long-term positioning, and they have contributed to another astounding quarter with incredibly strong top- and bottom-line growth.

However, the stock has increased in value substantially over the past year and is currently trading at high valuation multiples, which I believe makes investing today a difficult proposition. For this reason, I am reiterating my HOLD rating on the stock, despite it having outperformed since my previous coverage.

Business Description & Key Considerations:

Robinhood is a global financial ecosystem that operates with nearly 27 million funded customers and a third of a trillion dollars worth of assets under custody.

I'd like to begin by discussing some of the firm's recent product additions, along with upcoming projects that can spur growth moving forward. Robinhood is now enabling its users to open multiple separate brokerage accounts, which will allow traders to segregate their assets according to different strategies they may be employing.

Traders can also now short stocks directly through Robinhood, which is an innovative feature that I anticipate will be utilized often given the platform's extremely risk tolerant and speculative user base.

Robinhood Banking is another exciting product the company is beginning to roll out, which will offer users the ability to park cash in high yield checking and savings accounts, and the platform will include services related to estate planning and tax optimization.

One of the primary reasons that Robinhood is rolling out a banking solution to its users is that this product will likely limit the number of customer withdrawals from the firm's ecosystem, and it should also lead to steady increases in funded accounts over time.

Another project Robinhood has been working on is tokenized equities, with European users now being able to purchase tokens that represent ownership in U.S.-based companies. At the moment over 400 individual stocks and ETFs are available through this feature, and this number is expected to continue growing.

These tokens are currently issued to users on Arbitrum, which is an Ethereum Layer 2 blockchain; however, Robinhood is working on its own proprietary blockchain, which will soon be used by its members to trade these tokens. Also, tokenized equities are currently allowing Robinhood to earn more revenue per trade than when U.S. users trade stocks since EU citizens are forced to pay a 10 bps FX conversion fee to purchase tokens.

Another key catalyst for Robinhood's business is its expansion into prediction markets with its partners Kalshi and Polymarket. In Q3, over 2.3 billion contracts were traded, and around 2.5 billion swapped hands in just October of this year alone (signaling massive growth for Q4).

Furthermore, this business is already on track to deliver approximately $300 million in annualized revenue, and this should continue growing given that the prediction markets industry is expected to grow at a CAGR of around 28% over the next five years.

Users can now trade these contracts to bet on the outcomes of sporting events, elections, important economic prints, and other random world events as well. According to this source, Robinhood currently holds around 32.5% market share in the U.S. prediction markets trading sector, and I believe that this figure will likely continue to expand over the coming years.

I am also of the opinion that Robinhood's strategy to partner with companies like Kalshi and Polymarket is absolutely genius, as CEO Vlad Tenev has noted that he expects lots of competition among the popular exchanges in the space given the recent explosion in popularity of trading contracts.

Robinhood's competitive edge when it comes to offering users prediction markets trading is that it operates a platform where users can also trade crypto, stocks, and options, which will be nearly impossible for new entrants to replicate quickly and reliably.

Moving on, Robinhood has also launched its Ventures Fund, which is an attempt to further the company's stated mission of "democratizing finance" by allowing retail investors to gain exposure to privately held companies. This will be structured as a closed-end fund (CEF), and it is set to be listed under the ticker symbol RVI.

This announcement has come out after the company's somewhat disastrous release of tokens that represented fractional ownership in special purpose vehicles (SPVs) which held stakes in popular privately held companies like OpenAI and SpaceX.

While the intent here appears to be noble, there are several notable risk factors that I think are worth monitoring. First, as this article details, the CEF structure means that shares can trade at a massive premium to net asset value (NAV) if investor demand is high (which it probably will be given Robinhood's popularity).

The company's user base is pretty notorious for engaging in extremely risky trading activities, and if this fund is approved, I have no doubt in my mind that the fund will trade at many multiples of its underlying NAV.

On the bright side, upon examining the filing, Robinhood has decided that it will only be charging investors a management fee that is based on the net assets held in the fund, not the market value. This means that investors won't be grossly overcharged if and when shares begin to trade at a premium to NAV; however, I still don't believe that this product will lead to substantial returns for those who buy in.

Finally, I'd like to touch on Robinhood's somewhat recent acquisition of the global crypto exchange Bitstamp. The primary rationale behind making this deal was that it will allow the firm to serve large institutional clients and expand its crypto business overall.

An excellent strategic decision the company made that was related to this acquisition was the introduction of smart exchange routing, which essentially refers to a newly implemented algorithm that cross-checks multiple exchanges to find the lowest price for Robinhood users to execute trades.

This feature allows Robinhood to offer lower take rates for high volume traders in particular, which is leading to more "power users" transitioning to the platform. By onboarding more high-activity users, Robinhood will generate more order flow and will also have the opportunity to upsell its other high margin offerings as well.

Financials & Valuation:

Robinhood reported strong third-quarter results, with total net revenues doubling YoY to ~$1.3 billion and adjusted EBITDA growing over 175% YoY to $742 million (representing a 58% margin). Furthermore, the firm's net income jumped more than 270% YoY to $556 million, which translates to a 43.6% net margin.

Robinhood's transaction-based revenues remained the primary engine of growth for the business, increasing 129% YoY to $730 million. Net interest revenues also expanded rapidly, up 66% YoY to $456 million.

Options trading still remains the largest contributor to transaction-based revenue, with revenue climbing 50% YoY to $304 million in Q3. However, revenue attributable to crypto trading ballooned 339% YoY to $268 million, which indicates that this may soon leapfrog options as the largest sales driver.

Equities trading revenue was also a standout, rising 132% YoY to $86 million, and sales for the "Other" category (which includes fees from withdrawals and prediction markets contracts) skyrocketed 279% YoY to $72 million.

Net interest revenue also grew dramatically, coming in at $456 million (up 66% YoY). The largest source here was once again margin interest, which increased 84% YoY to $153 million. The firm also benefited substantially from higher customer balances, along with increased trading activity.

Additionally, fees from lending securities to institutional investors increased by around $68 million (or 358%) YoY as Robinhood expanded its fully paid securities lending business. Furthermore, interest on customer funds increased by around 69% YoY to $103 million, which was the second largest portion of net interest revenue.

Finally, Robinhood's other revenues doubled YoY to $88 million, with Robinhood Gold subscription revenue increasing 68% YoY to $47 million. Robinhood also achieved net deposits in Q3 of roughly $20.4 billion, bringing total platform assets to $333 billion.

Turning to the company's expense profile, total operating costs rose 31% YoY to $639 million. The primary drivers here were G&A expenses, which increased $52 million (39%) YoY to $185 million, along with marketing-related costs, which grew 73% YoY to $102 million. None of these increases are anything to worry about whatsoever in my opinion, given the high degree of operating leverage that is allowing the business to rapidly expand its margins and, in turn, its profitability.

Now let's turn to the valuation of the stock. Robinhood currently trades at over 53x forward earnings, which is over 350% higher than the sector median.

While the company does maintain a significantly higher long-term EPS growth rate when compared to others in the industry (20.6% compared to 11.4% on average), I struggle to see how much upside is left here given the apparently stretched multiples.

The stock is up over 200% over the past year alone and over 10x since the lows experienced during 2022-2023. To me this indicates excessive optimism from investors, and I would personally prefer to purchase shares at a lower price point.

Conclusion:

Robinhood remains one of the single most innovative and bold finance platforms in the world, and its recent product launches have strengthened its long term growth opportunities across a number of different areas.

Management's execution has been very impressive, as user growth continues to trend upward and the business has been able to translate solid revenue growth into even greater profit margin expansion.

Despite all of the positives here, I believe that the stock's run up over the past year means there is very little upside left for those who wish to invest today, and that is why I am rating shares of Robinhood a HOLD.

data.title: Tracking Chris Hohn's TCI Fund Management 13F Portfolio - Q3 2025 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to TCI Fund Management’s 13F portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on their regulatory 13F form filed on 11/14/2025. Please visit our Tracking Chris Hohn’s TCI Fund Management 13F Portfolio article for an idea on their investment philosophy and our last update for the fund’s moves during Q2 2025.

This quarter, TCI Fund Management’s 13F portfolio value increased from $50.71B to $52.70B. The number of positions remained steady at 9. The top three holdings are at ~61% while the top five are close to ~83% of the 13F assets: GE Aerospace, Visa, Microsoft, Moody’s, and S&P Global.

Note: In May, during an interview with Chris Hohn, it was disclosed that they hold significant stakes in Safran SA (OTCPK:SAFRY) and Aena SME SA (OTCPK:ANYYY).

Stake Increases:

Visa Inc. (V): The ~18% of the portfolio stake in Visa was built in 2020 at prices between ~$147 and ~$219. H2 2021 had seen a ~64% stake increase at prices between ~$196 and ~$249 while in the next quarter there was a ~14% reduction at prices between ~$191 and ~$235. The first three quarters of 2023 also saw a ~18% trimming. There was a ~15% stake increase during the last quarter at prices between ~$299 and ~$376. That was followed by a ~47% stake increase this quarter at prices between ~$329 and ~$360. The stock is now at ~$334.

Moody’s Corp (MCO): MCO is a top five ~12% position that has been in the portfolio since their first 13F filing in Q2 2015. At the time, the stake was already fairly large at 3.9M shares. Recent activity follows. H1 2022 saw a ~25% stake increase at prices between ~$254 and ~$386. That was followed with a similar increase in the two quarters through Q1 2023 at prices between ~$235 and ~$330. Q4 2023 saw a ~16% further increase at prices between ~$302 and ~$392. The stock currently trades well above those ranges at ~$487. There was a marginal increase during the last two quarters.

Note: They have a ~7.3% ownership stake in the business.

S&P Global Inc. (SPGI): The ~10% of the portfolio SPGI stake was first purchased in 2017 and built in 2019 at prices up to ~$275. Q1 2021 saw a ~150% stake increase at prices between ~$306 and ~$362 while in H2 2021 there was a ~37% selling at prices between ~$415 and ~$475. There was a ~80% stake increase in Q1 2022 at prices between ~$373 and ~$461. That was followed with another ~30% increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$316 and ~$415. Q2 2024 saw a ~15% further increase at prices between ~$408 and ~$449. The last quarter also saw a ~7% stake increase at prices between ~$427 and ~$528. The stock currently trades at ~$496. There was a marginal increase this quarter.

Stake Decreases:

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT): MSFT is a large (top three) ~16% of the portfolio stake established during Q4 2023 at prices between ~$313 and ~$383. There was a ~14% stake increase during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$394 and ~$467. That was followed by a ~17% stake increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$405 and ~$454. Q1 2025 also saw a ~24% stake increase at prices between ~$367 and ~$448. The stock currently trades well above those ranges at ~$486. This quarter saw a ~5% trimming.

Note: MSFT had a previous roundtrip. A ~12% MSFT stake was built in the 2017-18 timeframe at prices between ~$60 and ~$115. There was a ~50% selling in 2019 at prices between ~$100 and ~$160. There was a ~48% reduction during the two quarters through Q2 2023 at prices between ~$222 and ~$348. The stake was disposed in the next quarter at prices between ~$312 and ~$358.

Canadian Pacific (CP): The ~7% CP stake was purchased during 2018-19 at prices between ~$34 and ~$51. There was a minor increase during 2020 but since then the position had been kept steady. The stock currently trades at $72.09. There was a ~6% trimming this quarter.

Note: they have a ~5.2% ownership stake in the business.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL): GOOGL is a 3.51% of the portfolio position. It was first purchased in 2017 at prices between ~$40 and ~$55. Next year saw the stake built at prices between ~$49 and ~$62. There was a ~18% selling in Q1 2022 while Q3 2022 saw a ~10% increase. There was a ~75% reduction during H1 2023 at prices between ~$87 and ~$128. Q2 2024 saw a ~27% stake increase at prices between ~$152 and ~$187. There was a ~20% reduction during Q1 2025 at prices between ~$152 and ~$209. The last quarter also saw a ~10% trimming at prices between ~$143 and ~$183. This quarter saw another ~53% reduction at prices between ~$173 and ~$256. The stock currently trades at ~$320.

Canadian National Railway (CNI): CNI is currently at 3.36% of the portfolio. It was built in the 2018-19 timeframe at prices between ~$71 and ~$95. Q2 & Q3 2021 saw a ~75% stake increase at prices between ~$104 and ~$127. Q2 2023 also saw a ~11% increase. There was a ~10% trimming during Q3 2024 at prices between ~$110 and ~$123. That was followed by a ~16% selling in the next quarter at prices between ~$101 and ~$117. Q1 2025 also saw a ~9% trimming at prices between ~$94 and ~$108. That was followed by another ~15% selling during the last quarter at prices between ~$92 and ~$109. This quarter also saw a ~18% reduction at prices between ~$91 and ~$107. The stock currently trades at ~$94.

Kept Steady:

GE Aerospace (GE): GE is currently the largest position in the portfolio at ~27%. It was purchased during Q1 2023 at prices between ~$66 and ~$94. There was a ~60% stake increase in the next quarter at prices between ~$94 and ~$110. Q1 2024 saw another ~12% increase at prices between ~$99 and ~$144. There was a ~5% reduction during Q4 2024 while the last quarter saw a similar increase. The stock currently trades at ~$297.

Ferrovial SE (FER): FER is a ~2% of the portfolio position purchased during Q2 2024 at prices between ~$36 and ~$41. The stock currently trades well above that range at $65.77.

Source: John Vincent. Data constructed from TCI Fund Management’s 13F filings for Q2 2025 and Q3 2025.

data.title: Knight-Swift Transportation: Well-Positioned Fundamentals Highlight Its Cheapness
data.description:
Four months after my last analysis, the price momentum of Knight-Swift Transportation Holdings Inc. (KNX) remains hammered despite my buy rating. I understand the cautious market outlook due to stubborn inflation and tariff woes. The freight and logistics industry remains hammered amid softening demand and production decrease across various industries. Despite this, KNX remains robust and resilient, which further highlights its cheapness. Meanwhile, technicals are still flat but show some bullish tendencies.

KNX Q3 2025: Some Strengths Shown

Freight and logistics companies continue to experience softness as macroeconomic conditions remain volatile. Inflation is still stubborn, fueled by new tariffs. Yet, some players remain solid with their strategic business model, vast coverage, and brand recognition. One good example is Knight-Swift Transportation Holdings, Inc.

In Q3 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $1.93B, up by 2.7% YoY from $1.88B. One aspect to consider is the increased fuel surcharges despite weaker oil prices. And I think we can attribute it to the capacity constraints experienced by the less-than-truckload, or LTL market. I will explain this further later. Its main operations remain resilient as revenues excluding fuel surcharges rose by 2.4% YoY.

As expected, the LTL market remained the core revenue driver. KNX took advantage of the favorable LTL market conditions. Undercapacity meant lower competition and LTL supply. KNX expanded its LTL network capacity by increasing the number of tractors and trailers to accommodate more demand amid weaker supply. With that, its actual shipments per day rose by 14.2%. Its revenue per shipment and revenue per hundredweight also increased. So, its total LTL segment revenue reached $341M, up by 21.5% YoY. This helped it offset the weakness in the truckload, or TL, market with a revenue decrease of 2.1% YoY.

Moreover, the company worked on its efficiency to manage costs and expenses better. As you can see, it reduced its operating capacity in the truckload segment in line with the softer demand. The number of tractors and trailers decreased by more than 1,000 units each. This paid off because the average revenue per tractor increased by 4.8% YoY. This means that KNX became more productive and efficient. It also lowered its purchased transportation expenses to be in sync with the market dynamics.

However, it incurred impairments, which led to a substantial increase in costs and expenses. On a lighter note, it was still able to generate positive profits. If we exclude impairment, the operating margin will be 4.4%, flat but slightly higher than in the same quarter last year.

Risk Factors

As a player in freight and logistics, sticky inflation in the US remains a big challenge for KNX. At 3.0%, inflation is back to its 14-month high. This means that the general price level keeps increasing, resulting in softer consumer spending. This is why recent studies show that consumers may reduce their holiday spending by 5.3% YoY. This can be aggravated by new tariffs due to their inflationary nature. This adds more cost pressures to production, reducing overall market supply across various industries. In turn, the demand for LTL and TL services may remain affected. KNX may also have to increase salaries and driver insurance, so employees can cope with the rising prices. I would also like to highlight the rising insurance and claims expenses. In the other trucking stocks I've covered recently, they also experience the same thing. And I believe this is rampant in the market.

Meanwhile, oil prices may remain a double-edged sword. The price of Brent and WTI remains near their 12-month lows. And I believe they will remain at this level or at least below $65 since the market has already priced in the tensions that have taken place in previous months, OPEC continues to counter it with its recent supply hikes. On the negative side, this can reduce fuel surcharges. On the positive side, KNX may incur lower fuel and purchased transportation expenses.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite these potential risks, I believe that KNX has some insulation against headwinds.

1. LTL Undercapacity May Spur Its Revenue Growth. The skyrocketing inflation and driver shortages are the big problems that freight and logistics players have faced in recent years. The softening demand and capacity constraints hurt the revenues and margins of many LTL companies, especially the smaller ones and those with limited network capacity. Since LTL companies are capital-intensive and cost-sensitive, it's not surprising that many companies have already shut down. As of this writing, many trucking companies have already filed for bankruptcy. Even large companies like Dolche Truckload, Daniel Trucking, and Elite Carriers did it.

Now, how does KNX benefit from these trends? Since the Great Freight Recession in 2022, it seems that the whole industry has become survival of the fittest. The bankruptcies led to severe LTL undercapacity as more players exited. This may offer more opportunities for the stronger players like KNX to capture more and newer demand. They may get the customers of those who have already filed for bankruptcy. Considering this, the lower LTL supply offsets the lower LTL demand. This allows the market to set higher LTL rates. As we can see, the LTL PPI is already 269, the first time that the index exceeded 260 in ten years. This trend is reflected by the most recent performance of KNX as it enjoyed a higher LTL revenue per shipment. And although holiday spending may be lower than in 2024, the seasonal demand uptick may help sustain the current LTL PPI.

2. Interest Rate Cuts May Boost Demand. In only two months, the policy easing cycle has already cut the interest rate by 50 bps. Although it is still elevated relative to pre-pandemic levels, the rate cut may lead to some positive spillovers. It may increase liquidity in the economy. It may also boost borrower and investor confidence to increase economic activity. In turn, employment and wages may improve. This may help many households keep up with the rising prices and maintain their spending.

Moreover, the interest rate cut can lower the borrowing cost or interest expense of KNX to increase its profitability and liquidity. It can also make credit cards more affordable, which can support spending this holiday season.

3. Its Fundamentals Are Still Robust. KNX's debt level is still high, which can be concerning at first glance. However, we must understand that it's a capital-intensive company. Also, its maturity profile remains long because over 90% of the total amount will not mature within a year. To be more specific, $1.4B, or 60% of the total, has a maturity of five years and above. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 2.0x, which means that it only needs two years to repay the whole amount at once, even if it doesn't have to. Hence, it maintains enough capital buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

After revisiting its valuation, KNX remains cheaper today than in previous years. Using the same metrics, such as book value per share, sales per share, and cash per share, the stock is still trading below historical averages. P/B, for instance, shows that the stock trades at only 1.01x versus the five-year average of 1.21x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $52.88. If we add a 10% risk consideration, it will be $47.59. Either way, the stock is still reasonably cheap today, so it still has some upside potential.

For a more forward-looking approach, I decided to revisit its valuation using the Dividend Discount Model. I based its annualized dividend of $0.72 on the quarterly payment of $0.18. Its dividend growth rate is based on the average growth since 2021 to exclude the impact of the pandemic. Meanwhile, I set its risk premium at 6.5%, which is higher than the standard of 5.0% to price in more risks. Lastly, I estimated its Beta on MS Excel using the SLOPE function on the daily price change versus the S&P 500. With all these numbers, I derived a TP of $60.76. If I add a 10% risk consideration, it will become $54.68. Either way, KNX is still reasonably cheap today.

Technicals

KNX's technicals are still flat as momentum remains weak despite recent rebound attempts. The stock price trend coincides with the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which means that it is almost the same as the average in the past 50 and 200 days. Meanwhile, the 50-day SMA is geared slightly upward, showing some optimism in the market sentiments.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI show buying opportunities. The MACD line is still trending below the signal line within negative territory along with the histogram. This implies that selling volume still outpaces buying volume. Yet, their increasing trend shows increasing buying volume, which suggests that the number of buyers who see the current price as an ideal entry point is starting to increase. Its RSI may support it considering the sharp increase in it after the continued downtrend. It is still below the overbought region, so there is enough buying room at this point.

Conclusion

Knight-Swift Transportation Holdings, Inc. sustains its topline strength despite inflationary headwinds and tariff woes. Its robust balance sheet and strategic tractor and trailer management position it very well for the current TL and LTL market conditions. This highlights its cheap valuation and justifies upside. Technicals adhere to it as buying signals increase after the continued price downtrend for almost a year. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Xunlei: Has More Than Enough To Get Back On Track
data.description:
Xunlei Limited (XNET), a provider of digital entertainment, cloud computing, and distributed cloud services in China, has fallen off in the last two months or so after reaching a new multi-year high as recently as early October, something the release of the Q3 FY2025 report was not able to stop. However, there are several reasons why remaining bullish on XNET is the way to go.

Why Now May Be Good Time to Get in on XNET

A previous article from August 2025 written by myself pointed out how the stock was pulling back, which could be viewed as an opportunity to get in on a stock that had a lot going for it. XNET was exposed to potential risks as a result of tensions between the U.S. and China, which continue, but this was more than offset by a strengthening income statement and balance sheet. Accordingly, I rated XNET a buy in the article.

The above chart shows how the stock did go on to rebound from the pullback in August to reach a new multi-year high of $11.03 on October 7, but the chart also shows how the stock has declined in the weeks since then. This may turn out to be another opportunity to get in on a stock whose asking price has been slashed by 36.5% since the October high after the stock closed at $7.00 on November 24.

Furthermore, there is reason to believe the stock encountered support, which may be enough to put a halt to the decline of the past several weeks and send the stock higher. This is because the stock reversed course to turn higher after hitting an intraday low of $6.40 on November 21, which is roughly where a Fibonacci retracement level resides.

If we assume the October 2025 high of $11.03 was the culmination of a move upwards that started with the low of $1.83 in December 2024 and the decline since then was a retracement of this move upwards, then the 50% Fibonacci retracement of $1.83 to $11.03 is $6.43. This could be why the stock turned higher after hitting $6.40 to close the day higher at $6.71 on November 21.

However, the stock was very oversold when this happened, which likely contributed to the bounce, if only because the stock dropped so much in recent weeks. XNET hit support, but it is possible for support to be breached on a subsequent move lower. In other words, the bottom may not yet be in.

If this happens and support is breached, the stock could be heading for the next potential support level. This would be the next or 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of $1.83 to $11.03, or $5.34. Not only is the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement often the most heavily defended of potential support levels, but it would also put the stock close to the lower ascending trendline that has been in place since the stock started to move higher, shown in the above chart.

Whenever the stock came close to this trendline and was in danger of falling below it in the past 12 months, the stock went on to turn around to move north. XNET may not get to this point, but if it does, it would make for a very good entry point since there is a high chance the stock proceeds to move higher from then on.

Long XNET is Supported by Structural Shifts Taking Place

Encountering support is not the only reason for long XNET. The case for long XNET is based on the structural shift towards cloud use. At its core, XNET is really a provider of cloud services, and almost everything else, like the wide range of services and products or apps offered, is meant to facilitate demand for cloud services.

For instance, while XNET is known for many things like its sports/esports media platform, streaming, games, online advertising, and much more, it is probably best known as a platform for the sharing and storage of files of all sorts, which people can subscribe to at XNET. This creates cloud demand because one of the main advantages of the cloud is to increase data transfer speeds and to reduce latency, which people downloading stuff are always interested in.

This is just one of the advantages opting for the cloud offers. Using the cloud, for instance, offers redundancy in having online backups in case something happens offline to data. While XNET is a relatively small player and it is nowhere close to cloud giants like Tencent and Alibaba (BABA) in the cloud market in China or the likes of Amazon (AMZN) or Microsoft (MSFT) outside of China, it too benefits like these players from the growing uptake of cloud services that is taking place in not just China but worldwide.

Long XNET offers a way to play this structural and ongoing shift taking place. XNET is also a relatively diversified platform with multiple revenue streams that offers exposure to not just the cloud but other emerging fields that are believed to have great growth potential in the future, like blockchain.

The Q3 FY2025 Report Supports the Stock Going Higher

The latest report from XNET has yet to make a difference, but that does not take anything away from the strong results since it included enough to strengthen the conviction of those long XNET. XNET reported revenue grew 57.7% YoY to $126.4M in Q3 FY2025, which resulted in non-GAAP net income of $5.25M, or $0.09 per ADS, up from net income of $4.91M, or $0.08 per ADS in Q3 FY2024.

The YoY gain in net earnings was much less in comparison to revenue because it was skewed by net income in Q3 FY2024 benefiting from non-operating income like interest and other income. In terms of non-GAAP operating income, which is more representative of the actual performance of the company itself, XNET earned $3.76M in Q3 FY2025, way more than the $0.36M in Q3 FY2024.

All reporting segments reported growth. Revenue from subscriptions increased by 22.3% YoY to $40.7M, with the number of subscribers increasing from 5.51M to 6.56M YoY; revenue from cloud computing increased by 44.9% YoY to $36.6M, and revenue from live streaming and other services increased by 127.1% YoY to contribute the remaining $49.1M.

In terms of GAAP, XNET reported net income of $550.25M attributable to shareholders, or $8.60 per ADS, which includes a $545.84M unrealized gain attributable to change in the fair value of a long-term investment in Arashi Vision, better known for Insta360. The latter completed an IPO in June 2025, which caused the value of XNET’s stake to rocket higher.

This follows an earlier gain to the tune of $719.69M in the preceding quarter, or Q2 FY2025, which resulted in EPADS of $11.48 in Q2 FY2025. XNET finished the quarter with cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments of $284.1M, up from $275.6M in the preceding quarter and partially offset by total debt of $70.5M.

Guidance calls for revenue of $131-139M in Q4 FY2025, an increase of 60.7% YoY at the midpoint. FY2025 revenue is projected to end up at $453M, or an increase of 40.2% YoY, since Q1-Q3 FY2025 revenue totaled $318M, assuming Q4 FY2025 revenue of $135M, the midpoint of guidance. This looks to be enough for earnings to hit an estimated $0.45 per ADS by the end of FY2025.

If we use the latest projections, then FY2025 revenue growth of 40.2% is more than twice the CAGR of 19.5% in FY2020-2025. Not to mention that the numbers got stronger as the year went by, with Q4 FY2025 revenue forecast to grow at 60.7%, or more than three times the CAGR of 19.4% in the past five years. In other words, not only is XNET in the midst of a growth spurt, but growth is accelerating. Both are very bullish.

Why XNET is Arguably Undervalued

Still, there is no getting around the fact that the stock price has lost more than a third of its value since early October. However, while this may seem like a letdown, it could also be viewed as an opportunity to get in on a stock that is arguably undervalued at current valuations, especially when accounting for the gains in the income statement and balance sheet.

For instance, a look at the state of the latest balance sheet shows that XNET has a book value of $1,597.28M with total assets of $1,831.35M and total liabilities of $234.07M, which converts to a book value of $25.98 per ADS since the total number of ADS/ADRs is 61,470K. This means that with the stock trading at $7.00 per ADS, XNET is available at way less than book value with a price-to-book of just 0.27x.

However, there is a caveat here. Most of the value of the assets on the balance sheet is due to XNET’s stake in Arashi mentioned earlier, which was valued at $1,298.85M on the books. The issue here is that the stake’s value can fluctuate in real time depending on how Arashi trades on the Shanghai Stock Exchange. This means that while XNET reported big gains in the Q2 FY2025 and Q3 FY2025 reports in terms of GAAP, which boosted book value, it is possible this could reverse if the stake loses value for whatever reason.

If we were to, for instance, totally exclude the stake from the equation, XNET would have a book value of $298.43M, or $4.85 per ADS, which translates to a price-to-book of 1.35x, or more than one. However, this assumes the stake is worth absolutely nothing, which is unrealistic, especially considering how Insta360 is doing. While it is possible for Arashi to see its stock price drop, it is highly unlikely to drop far enough for XNET to not trade at less than book value.

If, for example, book value is to equal market cap for a price-to-book of one, total assets need to be $637.07M with the current market cap of $403M and total liabilities of $234.07M. Total assets stand at $298.43M without the Arashi stake, which means the stake needs to be worth at least $338.54M. This is just 26% of its current value on the books, or $1,298.85, which means the stake can drop 74% in value and XNET would still be valued at book value. If the stake is worth more than this amount, or $338.54M as it currently is, XNET will be trading at less than book value and thus undervalued based on the balance sheet.

XNET Could Also Be Undervalued Based on Future Growth

The balance sheet is not the only one making a case that XNET is undervalued because the same can be said of the income statement. It probably does not make sense to look at GAAP earnings because the number has been spiked by unrealized gains, but in terms of non-GAAP, which leaves out these gains, XNET earned $0.40 per ADS TTM. In contrast, XNET earned $19.97 per ADS in terms of GAAP.

This means XNET trades at a P/E ratio of 17.5x, with the stock last closing at $7.00. This multiple looks even better when viewed relative to future growth. According to the methodology espoused by, for instance, Peter Lynch, XNET can be deemed undervalued relative to growth if it grows earnings at a CAGR of more than 17.5% in the next 3-5 years.

This looks very achievable for XNET. As shown earlier, XNET is expected to grow by over 40% in FY2025, which includes over 60% in Q4 FY2025, or two and three times, respectively, the pace recorded in the past five years. It may be asking for a lot for XNET to maintain the present rate of growth in the coming years, but if we want to be conservative with projections, a CAGR of 30% in the next three years looks possible.

This implies FY2028 revenue of $995.2M, using the earlier estimate of $453M for FY2025 revenue as the base. If we then assume XNET manages to retain the current net profit margin of 6.2%, then FY2028 non-GAAP net income would come in at $61.7M. This would convert to net earnings of $1.00 per ADS if the number of ADS/ADRs stays at the current 61,470K.

Moreover, XNET has actually decreased the number of outstanding shares in previous years, and if this were to continue in the coming years, then the number of shares could total about 59.33M by the end of FY2028. Lowering the number of shares increases earnings per share, which suggests FY2028 EPADS is more likely to be $1.04 using the lower number of shares.

If XNET reaches $1.04 in FY2028, non-GAAP EPADS will have grown at a CAGR of 32.21% in FY2025-2028, using the estimate of $0.45 for FY2025 EPADS as the base. XNET only needs to grow earnings at a CAGR of 17.5% to be deemed fairly valued, and nearly twice as fast, or 32.21%, would make XNET undervalued relative to expected growth. Fair value for XNET would be more like $14.49 per ADS, assuming FY2025 EPADS of $0.45 and growing at a CAGR of 32.21%.

Investor Takeaways

XNET has experienced a huge drawdown in the stock. The stock has fallen for most of the last two months, which may come as a letdown after the strength XNET has displayed almost all year. However, it is worth mentioning that XNET has experienced these selloffs before, and XNET went on to not only recover but also go on to new heights.

The drawdown in the stock was in a way overdue after the big rally in the preceding months, which caused the stock price to multiply in value, and that is with the recent decline. Letting out some steam or taking a breather, especially after running for a long time, is healthy. Furthermore, the stock seems to have hit support, and even if that one is breached, another, even stronger support level looms. XNET could be in the early stages of a rebound with the backing of support.

In addition, XNET is arguably undervalued at current valuations after the recent discounting of the stock. In fact, XNET could be very undervalued, but this depends on what value is assigned to the stake XNET has in Arashi/Insta360, a fast-growing company, which reported in October that revenue in the first three quarters of FY2025 grew by 67.1% YoY to RMB6,610.94M, or about $930M.

The stake could have its value drop in half, and XNET would still be valued at less than book value. Furthermore, it is not just the balance sheet saying XNET deserves a higher valuation; the income statement suggests the same. According to the latest guidance in the recently released Q3 FY2025 report, FY2025 revenue is likely to grow by over 40% YoY.

Even if we were to temper our expectations and revenue were to grow at a lesser pace in the next few years, say 30%, which is less than half the 60+% Q4 FY2025 revenue guidance is calling for, XNET can grow earnings at a pace that warrants a much higher valuation of closer to the mid-teens, as detailed earlier.

I am therefore bullish on XNET with everything above in mind. The recent slide in the stock is not pleasing, but it can also be viewed as an opportunity to get in on shares at a much lower asking price, especially if we factor in all the attributes XNET has going for it, especially as a play on the growth in cloud demand, which the latest report shows is well justified. Both the income statement and the balance sheet point to higher share prices in the future. XNET has multiple pathways at its disposal. Chances are XNET will get there.

The Fund delivered strong absolute and relative performance in the third quarter, driven by robust stock selection in biotechnology, large-cap pharmaceuticals and select diagnostics. The Fed's rate cut in September improved risk appetite, supporting gains in innovative therapeutics, despite healthcare lagging broader equity indices. Abivax (ABVX) rose after reporting encouraging clinical trial results for obefazimod, its treatment for ulcerative colitis, and completing a successful capital raise that reduced financing risk. Meanwhile, uniQure (QURE) benefited from renewed optimism in gene therapy, with progress in its CSL Behring collaboration and programmes for Huntington's disease and epilepsy. AbbVie (ABBV) posted double-digit growth in Skyrizi and Rinvoq, with management reaffirming an expansion in margins despite inflationary pressures.

Detractors were limited, with modest underperformance from Inspire Medical Systems (INSP) and Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) due to normalising elective procedures and hospital budget constraints. Also, diagnostics company DiaSorin (DSRNY) weakened slightly amid uneven test volumes and subdued small-cap sentiment.

Market review

Global equities ended the quarter higher, supported by strong corporate earnings and the US Federal Reserve (Fed)'s widely anticipated first 25 basis-point rate cut of the year in September. Early tariff-related jitters faded as hopes for broader trade agreements between the US and its key trading partners built, and sentiment was lifted by the 90-day extension of the US-China tariff truce to 10 November. Softer US labour market data and signs of easing inflation strengthened investor conviction that the Fed could implement further interest-rate cuts in the coming months, driving government bond yields lower. A cautiously dovish message from Fed Chair Jerome Powell reinforced this view, and he reaffirmed the central bank's independence shortly before President Trump sought to dismiss Governor Lisa Cook over alleged mortgage fraud.

Although President Trump announced 100% tariffs on branded and patented pharmaceutical imports, the level was lower than the 200-250% initially feared; moreover, companies building manufacturing facilities in the US are exempt, while existing EU-US trade arrangements already cap European pharmaceutical tariffs at 15%. In late September, President Trump announced a Gaza peace plan endorsed by Israel but still under review by Hamas, with no immediate effect on markets or policy. At the end of the quarter, the US government partially shut down for the first time in nearly seven years after Democrats and Republicans failed to reach a funding deal, forcing hundreds of thousands of federal workers into furlough and halting key government services. The resulting loss of output and income is expected to weigh on short-term growth, while delays to key data releases could weigh on investor confidence.

The performance of the global healthcare sector notably lagged that of global equities over the quarter. Cyclical sectors such as communication services, information technology, materials and consumer discretionary outperformed as investors rotated towards growth areas given softening economic data and slowing inflation, which fuelled expectations of interest-rate cuts. Materials also drew support from improved US-China trade sentiment after the extension of a 90-day tariff freeze, while higher gold prices reflected ongoing economic and geopolitical uncertainty. In contrast, defensive sectors including consumer staples, utilities and real estate underperformed as risk appetite increased. Financials and industrials likewise underperformed, held back by softer economic data and waning business confidence. Energy broadly matched the performance of global equities, with oil prices ending flat as signs of softening demand, reflected in rising crude inventories, were offset by geopolitical tensions that raised supply concerns.

Activity

Portfolio activity focused on adding high-conviction companies at attractive valuations. New positions were initiated in CVS Health (CVS), Diasorin, Renalytix (OTC:RNLXY), Alcon (ALC), Diaceutics, Prenetics Global (PRE) and OSE Immuno, targeting undervalued franchises with clear catalysts. The Fund reduced exposure to smaller biotechnology holdings with delayed catalysts, reallocating capital to companies like AbbVie and Novo Nordisk (NVO) to lock in gains while maintaining core exposure. This modestly increased weightings in diagnostics and diversified healthcare while retaining a biotechnology tilt.

Outlook & strategy

As the global trade shock has moderated and the risk of a tariff spiral has receded, the probability of a US recession has fallen. However, uncertainty has not dissipated, with US trade and fiscal policy likely to persist as sources of volatility both for the global economy and financial markets. We therefore reiterate the importance of exposure to a diverse set of high-quality global opportunities.

In general, the near- and long-term outlooks for healthcare companies remain favourable. Long-term demographic trends of an aging population should continue to support the growing demand for new healthcare products and therapies. Due to the healthcare sector's generally defensive characteristics, its relative underperformance in the short term should position it favourably, especially if the US and global economies slow down.

data.title: Palantir: No Rational Reason To Own It Besides Momentum
data.description:
About six months ago, I wrote a piece on Palantir (PLTR) where I highlighted a strategy based on the riding-the-bull thesis without being thrown off, which involved following a strategy based on reducing exposure during high volatility and weak trends, significantly outperforming just by holding the stock. The aim was to avoid the painful drawdowns that an absurdly rich stock like PLTR could eventually offer.

And in short, the way to survive an extreme convexity thesis is not to ignore risk, but to manage the trend regime.

I would argue that this point has become even more relevant now, in the midst of an environment in which exactly the same symptoms that motivated that earlier thesis are replicating. In the absence of a reliable "fair multiple" and when inaccurate projections tend to depend on still distant options, the only real actionable instrument for investing falls to positioning.

That said, under the argument that Palantir should be treated as a momentum discipline asset, not as a conventional valuation story, what we see today in terms of (1) divergence of moving averages; (2) short-term stress; (3) absence of capitulation; and (4) the back and forth of flows reinforces the playbook of repeating the trend, reducing positions in weak regimes and increasing them only when the tape confirms recovery.

Everyone's Right, Everyone's Uncomfortable

Setting the stage, Palantir's fundamentals today can be considered insane, as can its valuation. Therefore, the only honest way I see to frame Palantir now would be through positioning and momentum.

As a quick recap, Palantir's 3Q25 brought the following highlights: (1) Rule of 40 at 114% (revenue growth 64% YoY + operating margins at 50%); (2) U.S. commercial growing 121% YoY, and U.S. overall growing 77% YoY; (3) massive FCF at $1.1 billion and buyback firepower ($373.8 million repurchased in the last twelve months, but mostly to offset SBC dilution).

According to the sell-side view, the interpretation of the moment is basically based on "best software company" at the moment, but trading at 87x forward sales and 224x forward earnings, this is almost nonsensical. This shows that no one disagrees with the business, but that the whole debate revolves around how much future growth is already baked into the price.

This leads me to classify Palantir's thesis as primarily a momentum asset, not a valuation asset.

The Valuation Problem

As Seeking Alpha's rates show, Palantir's multiples are so absurd that they trade ~5x richer than high-quality infra software peers. This territory is so unprecedented that there is no way to anchor a "fair" multiple in this company's thesis.

Today's valuation is based on multiple years of 50%+ revenue growth and at least 50% operating margins—and obviously a macro resilient enough to support this outlook.

This undoubtedly causes a "battleground" dynamic where, on one side, Palantir is labeled as the best AI operator worth a premium, and on the other, this is a fantastic company but trading at a fantasy price. In reality, I believe that both sides may be overexposing themselves, just handicapping crowd psychology.

In my last thesis, written on Palantir, I outlined what could be embedded in a $1 trillion valuation for the company, based on the capitalization of options in the thesis that can best explain a 200x-plus multiple.

Quickly put into context, these optionalities were based on five key points: (1) general-purpose corporate AI, with AIP at the heart of the thesis being universally adopted among enterprises; (2) the expansion of government contracts in emerging countries and NATO; (3) capitalization under new vertical sectors such as healthcare and finance; (4) deepen internationalization mainly in Europe and Asia; and (5) partnerships and acquisitions potentially integrating Palantir with other software.

Considering the respective addressable markets for each of these initiatives, and putting the variables of cost of execution, time of realization, uncertainty of a payoff, and a reasonable risk-free rate with the current one, I had estimated $1.1 trillion of extra options to be incorporated into Palantir's thesis, already over what the company delivers today—with more than half of that amount linked to an accessible TAM for AIP by 2034 in the trillions of dollars as an average baseline. As a reference, third-party firms say that AI in general could add something like $15-16 tri/year to world GDP by 2030.

Of course, this is an assumption, with a high dose of speculation, and potentially adjusted to perfection. But the purpose of showing this path is precisely an exercise in how one could justify (or not justify) a monstrously high multiple for an unprecedented and unanchored thesis.

Momentum as the Real Thesis

If it's practically impossible to reach a common denominator to justify valuations convincingly, let's get down to the nitty-gritty of Palantir's thesis and the real reason why investors keep buying the thesis and sustaining the high premium: momentum.

Since momentum will tend to guide investors in and out of this thesis above all else, let's look at the current state of the trend.

Palantir recently hit all-time highs of ~$207 per share and suffered a pullback to $160s-not necessarily justified by fundamentals, as 3Q25 earnings were pristine, to be clear. The problem is that in the short term, the risk of a choppy regime occurs as valuation digests. Keep in mind that flows and narrative drive as much as fundamentals, and a PLTR is a "story vehicle" for AI.

With that, let's split into sub-profiles: early bulls vs. late chasers.

For those who are in from much lower (e.g., teens), they are probably sitting on four-digit percentage gains, and I believe the risk of giving back too much if the regime changes. Selling covered calls after big rallies or monitoring short-term moving averages and trimming on vertical spikes could be the case.

But essentially, I would only consider zeroing the position in a scenario of price breaking below the 50-day SMA and (1) failing to regain momentum in 2 to 3 weeks; and (2) volume on red days being higher than on Green days. In theory, this would be the biggest sign that big money is distributing, not accumulating, and every major drawdown tends to start that way. Or, if Palantir ever closes 2-3 weeks below the 200-day SMA, that would show that it's no longer in a secular uptrend.

As the chart below shows, Palantir's price at the time of writing, at $161.64, is below the 20-day and 50-day SMA, proving to be a no-buy zone for a volatile stock. For stubborn bulls, buying in this setup would be fighting with momentum, and the chance of success is low. Generally, good buy setups move above the 20-day SMA, or else when the 20-day SMA crosses above the 50-day SMA (confirmation).

Note also that the volume (57.88 million) and the 30-day average volume (57.20 million) show a normalization, showing no fear of capitulation yet, and no strong accumulation—arguably good for building a base, but not a buy trigger.

So, I would say that ideally, the perfect time to go long on Palantir for late chasers mitigating risk, would be if (1) price closes above the 20-day SMA (first sign of sellers exhaustion); (2) price closes above the 50-day SMA (confirmation); (3) volume spikes above ~70 million, indicating smart money stepping in. It gets even better if a position is built up gradually according to the SMA's unfolding as described.

Conclusion

I believe that for those who are carrying Palantir with some relevant gain, the worst mistake now would be to get rid of the position out of emotion, just as it would be irresponsible to ignore signs of a regime change.

The current moment, technically, is not a "sell everything" period, but one that requires some level of monitoring discipline. The long-term trend remains intact, which supports a structural bull case, but the short term is weakened—below the 20- and 50-day SMAs, with no signs of volume re-acceleration.

A zero-position scenario would only make sense under a technical confirmation of institutional distribution, breaking the 50-day with failure to return, and then breaking the 200-day. Until then, tactical trims, covered calls, and gradual reductions make more sense than an early full exit.

But overall, I would summarize the thesis by pointing out that at the moment, there is no sensible reason in the short to medium term to own Palantir besides momentum-which is exactly why investors need a clear playbook, whether it's long, flat, or tempted to bet against it.

data.title: John Hancock Emerging Markets Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The fund's benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, posted a robust gain in the third quarter.
  • The fund underperformed the benchmark.
  • Positioning in Indonesia and Taiwan detracted from results.

Market review and outlook

Emerging-market stocks performed very well and strongly outpaced developed markets in the quarter. A favorable backdrop of positive global growth, falling interest rates, and a general lack of negative news flow fueled a surge in investors' appetite for risk. While all regions posted gains, Asia led the way thanks to impressive returns for China, South Korea, and Taiwan. The Latin America region made a further contribution to index returns.

Contributors and detractors

An overweight in Indonesia, together with stock selection in the country, detracted from relative performance. Indonesian stocks came under pressure from civil unrest, as well as political and fiscal uncertainty, leading to a negative return for the fund's position in Bank Mandiri Tbk. Stock selection in Taiwan also hurt performance. The shortfall was largely due to a position in eMemory Technology, Inc., which reported weaker-than-expected results. Stock selection in Brazil was also an additional detractor. The e-commerce giant MercadoLibre, Inc. (MELI), which introduced extremely generous delivery options to defend its market position—a decision that adversely affected the company's profitability—was the primary detractor.

Selection in South Korea contributed to performance. The country's technology sector was buoyed by continued enthusiasm about the AI trade, as well as the potential for improving corporate governance. Samsung Electronics Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF) was the key driver of the fund's positive performance in this market. An overweight in Greece also contributed thanks to strong results from our positions in the banking sector. Outside of these two countries, the Chinese stocks Contemporary Amperex Technology Co., Ltd., Tencent Holdings Ltd. (OTCPK:TCEHY), and Trip.com Group Ltd. (TCOM) were among top contributors during the period.

Portfolio changes

Our stock selection process remains grounded in thorough bottom-up analysis that seeks to identify companies that can generate earnings growth despite top-down challenges.

Tariffs remain a key consideration for emerging-market investors. We think it's reasonable to expect that GDP growth and aggregate earnings will slow across the asset class, not least due to over-ordering ahead of the imposition of tariffs. Many companies are unable to pass the full cost of tariffs on to their customers, which could have an adverse effect on profits.

China remains the focal point of U.S. trade policy, and we may see clarity on final tariff levels before year end. Policy support, positive sentiment surrounding China's homegrown advances in AI, and undemanding valuations have combined to fuel a market recovery. However, slower global trade could hinder growth in the nation's economy. The trade issue could present an interesting opportunity in Brazil since the effect of the reciprocal tariffs should be small, and potential rate cuts will likely be far more influential for growth. Similarly, India has the fiscal, monetary, and external buffers to manage any shocks. We therefore believe India's long-term structural growth story remains intact, albeit with some short-term challenges.

Despite the headwind of slower global growth and the possible risk to earnings, we remain optimistic about the outlook for emerging-market stocks. A number of countries have already started cutting interest rates, which helps economic growth and lowers the cost of capital. Further rate cuts in the United States—should they materialize—and continued weakness in the U.S. dollar can also be expected to be supportive for the asset class. Still, the emerging markets continue to trade at a compelling discount relative to their developed-market peers. In our view, this indicates a broad range of opportunities for individual stock selection.

data.title: Protection From Tariffs, Boost From AI, Market-Beating Returns: Why Enbridge Is Set To Win
data.description:
Introduction

All financial figures are expressed in CAD, unless otherwise stated.

Enbridge (ENB) has been a stock I have long wanted to cover, as it is an obvious expansion of the research I am carrying out about the key supply chain implications of the AI revolution. I started publishing the outcomes of my research back in August, with the article "Capex Nation: Investment Themes For A Decade". I tried to explain why, even though the big winner now seems to be Nvidia (NVDA), chips don't seem to be the best possible bet to benefit and earn gains from this revolution. Chips, in fact, experience quick obsolescence and are, in any case, cyclical. There are, however, many important bottlenecks and key spots of the whole AI build-out process that seem able to generate constantly growing cash flows. Enbridge is part of this, as one of the biggest consequences of AI is the increased hunger for energy. Since we are not ready to produce enough energy through renewables or nuclear power plants, I am particularly bullish on natural gas. The Alerian MLP ETF gives exposure to MLPs that operate and invest in energy infrastructure. But there are also single stocks that are particularly set to benefit from what could become a gas rush with no precedents. I have already written about Energy Transfer and its enticing 8% yield, or about Duke Energy (DUK) and the Southeast power demand boom. I also argued that as AI builds, ONEOK bills. Now, it is time to look at one of the undisputable energy infrastructure champions in North America: Enbridge, which moves around 30% of the crude oil that is produced in North America, 65% of Canadian exports to the U.S., 40% of the oil the U.S. imports, and more or less 25% of the oil that North America exports. Clearly, Enbridge is not exposed to tariffs. As a side note, if you are bullish on LNG exports as I am, well, Enbridge is connected to every USGC (U.S. Gulf Coast) LNG terminal.

Enbridge: Business Overview

Enbridge's business model might seem pretty simple at first glance: the company states in its Annual Report that it is an energy infrastructure company that transports across Canada and the U.S. crude oil, many other liquid hydrocarbons, and natural gas. On top of this, Enbridge also runs a natural gas utility business (Enbridge serves fast-growing areas with almost 7M customers, delivering approximately 9.1 Bcf/d) in some areas of North America and a renewable power generation unit. Interestingly, Enbridge's renewable assets (mainly wind and solar) are also present in Europe (the U.K., France, and Germany). This is why we will see Enbridge report its results under four segments: liquid pipelines, gas transmission, gas distribution and storage, and renewable power. In the slide below, we can see both Enbridge's infrastructure in North America and the growth opportunities for each segment that Enbridge forecasts to materialize through 2030. The largest is gas transmission.

As Enbridge itself explains, its assets "have reliably generated low-risk, resilient cash flows through many different commodity, economic, and geopolitical environments". Yes, unlike what we may think at first, Enbridge is not really exposed to commodity price volatility due to a take-or-pay contract structure.

For example, for its Mainline System (a prime infrastructure that carries around 3.2 mmbpd), Enbridge has negotiated a tolling settlement that includes initial fixed tolls, plus some surcharges, and then distance tolls are added to end with a financial performance collar that incentivizes Enbridge to optimize its efficiency, while making sure the Mainline System earns stable returns between 11% and 14.5%. Enbridge always manages its assets actively, dismissing poor-performing ones to invest in better-yielding projects or companies (M&A is part of Enbridge's activity). For example, Enbridge has focused on improving its Permian footprint to gain better exposure to crude oil and natural gas output from this key basin.

Natural Gas Demand

I won't repeat what I have often shown in the past few months. The Annual Energy Outlook 2025, published by the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA), shows how natural gas keeps being a key commodity. Moreover, the EIA also calculated that natural gas production through 2050 (base year 2022) will grow by over 50% on the Gulf Coast and in the Southwest, as global demand for both natural gas and LNG will grow, incentivizing the building of new export facilities. Enbridge confirms that North America will see its natural gas demand grow 14% through 2040, thanks to the new opportunity created by data centers and the ongoing trend of coal-to-gas switching, which is estimated to be a massive 24 Bcf/d opportunity.

By the way, Enbridge says that 29 new data centers are being built within 50 mi of its system and that 78 coal plants are within 50 mi of its system.

So, guess where Enbridge is investing? Yes, gas. Through 2029, most of its capital will be allocated toward gas transmission and gas distribution, while maintaining a debt/EBITDA leverage between 4.5x and 5.0x (for energy infrastructure companies, it is an acceptable leverage).

Enbridge's Financials

Enbridge's top line is heavy, with the company set to clearly break the $60B threshold in revenues this year ($65B could also be achievable). However, with energy infrastructure companies, we usually focus more on the EBITDA as the starting line. This year, the company expects to achieve an adj. EBITDA between $19.4B and $20B. The distributable cash flow per share should be in the range between $5.50 and $5.90. In 2026, the company expects to keep growing its EBITDA in the 7-9% range, with EPS growing between 4% and 6%, and a dividend increase of around 3%.

The company reports in its balance sheet LT debt of $72.4B, while it holds in cash "only" $1B. This is normal: Enbridge generates billions in FCF, so it doesn't have to keep a lot of cash at hand like other companies in different industries must (think about the main automakers that have to keep $10 or more in cash). As Enbridge's management confirmed during the earnings call, the company is sticking to its commitment of keeping the debt/EBITDA ratio below 5x. Moreover, we also learned that business is great for the company since its assets are being, for the most part, completely utilized. This means that cash flow generation is at its peak and that fixed costs weigh less on the overall cost structure.

Enbridge's status as a Dividend Aristocrat makes it a dividend investors' favorite, and for a reason. Its dividend policy aims at a 60%-70% of the company's DCF payout range. In the past 5 years, the company returned to its shareholders around $35B. For the next 5 years, the goal is $40-$45B. Moreover, its dividends have not been a hurdle to the total performance. Since 2005, Enbridge has delivered a yearly 12% TSR, beating the market. Enbridge has gone through volatile times, but it surely presents itself as a long-term holding able to offer nice rewards to long-term shareholders. The company's dividend increases moderately and is sometimes below the inflation rate. But Enbridge is in for the long run and prefers to protect its streak of yearly dividend increases rather than facing the risk of a dividend cut.

Enbridge's Preferred Shares

Enbridge has issued a variety of preferred shares (20 at the time of writing). This makes sense because the company needs capital, and through these shares, it can have easy access to capital at decent rates. When investors choose to invest in preferred shares, they are choosing to earn a higher yield while giving up some of the capital gains that common shares can offer. These shares pay fixed dividends until a reset date. Enbridge lists on its investor relations webpage all the reset dates and the dividend increases. Usually, after the reset date, the new rate is the result of the 5-year Canada bond yield and a fixed spread, which can range between 2.5% and 4% (for example, ENB.PR.F: CA had its fixed rate period from January 18th, 2012, to June 1st, 2018, and paid a $0.25 per quarter, which was equal to a 4% annual yield; after June 1st, 2018, it can be converted into a G shares that pay a new yield for 5 years based on the Canadian 5-year bond and a 2.51% spread). There are also fixed-to-floating shares that can be interesting when short-term rates move up faster than LT yields. However, I don't think this will be the scenario going forward. If one's goal is only income, ENB offers a wide range of preferred shares, but I think that common shares are a better option: they offer exposure to the gas megatrend while also paying a nice 6.5% yield.

Risks

No investment comes with zero risk. The first one is that Enbridge, though aligned with the industry, is a leveraged business. This means that if we believe that inflation will run hot once again, driving the Fed and other central banks to hike interest rates, then Enbridge will sail through unfavorable financing times. The second risk is linked to the long-term outlook for key commodities such as oil and natural gas. While I have explained why I am bullish on natural gas, some believe that oil will inevitably experience declining demand down the road due to increased ESG pressure. The energy transition could therefore be an issue, even though I think it won't truly materialize for a decade or more. There is another risk linked to oil and gas: if volumes suddenly drop because of a major recession, Enbridge's fee-based contracts won't fully protect the company, especially when they come to maturity and must be renewed. The last risk is something we would never have imagined just a couple of years ago: there is a geopolitical risk that has materialized as the Canada-U.S. relationship is experiencing some tensions. While I see it unlikely for the two countries to reduce cross-border activities, we can't fully rule this possibility out.

Enbridge's Valuation

How much is Enbridge worth? Right now, the stock trades at a fwd PE of 21 and a fwd EV/EBITDA ratio of 13. Both metrics contribute to its overall valuation grade of a D. The stock seems a bit pricey. But is it? Let's use a DCF model. I plugged in the financial metrics already converted into USD, so that the outcome is a share price in USD, which makes things easier for us on Seeking Alpha. I used the TTM metrics. My assumption is that the company will end this fiscal year with $3.55B in FCF (USD). Considering that gas is going to grow and that the company's assets are fully used, I think it is a very moderate assumption to expect Enbridge's FCF to grow at 5% per year through 2030. My perpetual growth rate is now 2.5% because I expect long-term inflation to stay above 2% for a while, as I have explained in the article "The 3% World". The outcome is that Enbridge is fairly priced, with the intrinsic value being $46.70, less than 2% below the current price. Now, when we reach a fair price, this doesn't mean the stock is not a buy. A fair price means we are paying something adequate and well-balanced for what we are getting in exchange. So, when a stock enters fair price territory, it means it enters buy territory. Depending on our portfolio management strategies, we can discuss the margin of safety we want to have. Probably, there can be better opportunities. If the stock dipped to $42 or below, we would surely have a wider margin of safety. But at Enbridge's current price, the deal is overall fair. One strategy could be to initiate a position and then add regularly to it for several months, deploying capital over time and eventually dollar-cost-averaging.

Conclusion

In an expensive market, I think Enbridge is an attractive buy with a long track record of market-beating returns, hefty dividends, and exposure to massive megatrends that will play in its favor. Enbridge is one of the key players, if not a true cornerstone, of North American energy infrastructure. Its ability to generate steady and growing cash flows through many economic conditions makes it an extremely attractive candidate for many different portfolio management strategies. While some risks, such as its leverage and commodity demand trends, remain, the company is also clearly positioned to benefit from the new gas megatrend that will power a big part of the AI revolution. Therefore, I think Enbridge offers a very good mix of potential capital appreciation and high yield.

data.title: American Century Small Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio Review

U.S. stocks posted solid gains. Equities rose, with some reaching record levels, contributing to strong year-to-date performance. Markets continued to weigh concerns about the impact of tariffs on growth and inflation but were buoyed by a resurgence of technology stocks later in the period, including artificial intelligence (AI)-related shares, and a Federal Reserve (Fed) interest rate reduction in September.

Small-cap stocks outperformed. Small-cap stocks led performance, recovering from weakness earlier in the year. Large-cap stocks also posted solid returns, while midsize companies registered more modest gains. Growth stocks outpaced value stocks among large-cap companies but lagged in the mid- and small-cap segments.

Industrials hampered performance. Stock choices in the sector detracted. Lack of exposure to Bloom Energy was a key detractor.

Information technology weighed on results. Our sector performance lagged due to stock selection, especially in the semiconductors and semiconductor equipment and software industries. We believe the sector was driven by AI across technology hardware and speculation about quantum computing stocks.

Utilities benefited performance. Stock choices in the sector were positive, helped by independent power and renewable electric producers. We believe electric utilities and power producers generally outperformed, driven by the need for power for AI computation. Talen Energy (TLN) was a significant contributor.

Key Contributors

Talen Energy. This is an independent power producer with nuclear reactors. Talen has been a first mover, building an on-site data center shell behind the meter at its Susquehanna nuclear plant campus. We believe it has successfully navigated some regulatory hurdles and has an even better deal with Amazon Web Services than originally announced.

Mineralys Therapeutics. This clinical-stage pharmaceutical company rose, benefiting from a major competitor's clinical data that showed inferior efficacy data compared with Mineralys' lead asset.

Badger Meter (BMI). This manufacturer of water-related products selling primarily into U.S. water utility end markets missed expectations. Our lack of exposure was beneficial.

Key Detractors

Bloom Energy. The stock of this alternative energy producer outperformed, and our lack of exposure detracted.

Kratos Defense & Security Solutions (KTOS). We did not own this defense and aerospace company whose stock outperformed.

Albany International. Our research indicated that estimated contract completion changes led to a material earnings miss in the third quarter for this small industrial conglomerate with aerospace and machinery segments.

Notable Trades

Sterling Infrastructure (STRL). We established a position in this infrastructure construction contractor. The company has strong exposure to data center build-out, which we believe is driving most of the capital spending in the U.S. currently.

CECO Environmental (CECE). We established a new position in this air purification and heating and air conditioning manufacturer. We believe CECO is benefiting from data center and power generation demand, which is seeing rapid growth from the artificial intelligence computing build-out.

Casella Waste Systems (CWST). We sold our position in this solid waste company as we believed better acceleration in other industrial segments was a better process fit than Casella Waste Systems.

Brinker International (EAT). The casual dining operator posted strong quarterly results, but with the same-store sales comparison getting progressively worse, we exited the position as we believed our thesis was met.

Portfolio Positioning

Our process is designed to identify companies producing attractive, sustainable earnings growth. We seek to reduce unintended risks and align the portfolio with company-specific characteristics that we believe will be rewarded over time. As a result of this approach, our sector and industry allocations reflect where we are finding opportunities at a given time.

Uncertainty remains. When we peel the onion, we believe the macroeconomic picture hasn't been giving us signs that all is well, despite the stock market rally. The labor market has continued to cool, and the deteriorating job backdrop has shown up in survey data related to consumer confidence and in manufacturing. Consumers remained most concerned about prices and inflation. Inflation has been sticky, with core prices still well above the Fed's target.

Straddling the line between cyclical and defensive positioning. Given the evolving macroeconomic backdrop, Fed policy uncertainty, tariff uncertainty and the crowded artificial intelligence trade, we have been straddling the line between cyclical and defensive positioning.

Our sector overweights and underweights remain narrow relative to the benchmark. We allow individual stock selection to drive our sector allocation decisions.

Our process is ongoing. We continue to evaluate our positioning regularly, while applying our bottom-up process to find what we believe are the best opportunities.
